original_abstract:
This paper explores the application of single-layer and deep convolutional neural networks for the analysis of remote sensing data. Applying supervised convolutional architectures directly to multi- and hyperspectral imagery is often problematic due to the high dimensionality of the data and the scarcity of labeled training samples. To address these challenges, we propose a greedy layer-wise unsupervised pre-training strategy powered by a highly efficient algorithm for learning sparse features. Our approach is grounded in sparse representation theory and simultaneously enforces both population and lifetime sparsity. We demonstrate the expressive power of these learned representations across several diverse scenarios, including aerial scene classification, land-use mapping in very high resolution (VHR) imagery, and land-cover classification from multi- and hyperspectral datasets. 

In our experiments, the proposed method significantly outperforms standard dimensionality reduction techniques like Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and kernel PCA, as well as several state-of-the-art algorithms for aerial classification, all while maintaining high computational efficiency. Our results indicate that while single-layer convolutional networks can extract potent discriminative features by accounting for spatial context in the receptive field, they are best suited for tasks requiring high-resolution, granular detail. In contrast, deep architectures offer superior performance by capturing increasingly complex levels of abstraction through their hierarchical feature structure.
humanized_abstract:
In this paper, we investigate the application of single layer and deep convolutional neural networks for remote sensing data analysis. While, supervised convolutional networks do not work directly on multi- and hyperspectral data due to their high dimensionality and the limited amount of available labeled training samples, we propose a new greedy layer-wise unsupervised pre-training approach (labeled as SFE) facilitated by an highly efficient algorithm for learning sparse features. The new feature learning algorithm exploits the benefits of sparse representation theory, while enforcing both population and lifetime sparsity. In the experimental part, the representativeness of the learned features is validated on a variety of different tasks, including aerial scene classification, land-use classification using very high resolution (VHR) images, as well as multi- and hyperspectral data.

In our experiments, the proposed method consistently and substantially outperforms baseline methods including PCA, kernel PCA, and other state-of-the-art methods designed for aerial classification in addition to being very fast. We also explain why single layer convolutional networks (while able to produce strongly discriminative features with local reception fields that capture spatial context) are only practical for high resolution (pixel level) fine grain classification, and that deeper networks can also learn to detect more abstract features which enhances their performance.

document.output:

While, supervised convolutional networks do not work directly on multi- and hyperspectral data due to their high dimensionality and the limited amount of available labeled training samples, we propose a new greedy layer-wise unsupervised pre-training approach (labeled as SFE) facilitated by an highly efficient algorithm for learning sparse features. The new feature learning algorithm exploits the benefits of sparse representation theory, while enforcing both population and lifetime sparsity. In the experimental part, the representativeness of the learned features is validated on a variety of different tasks, including aerial scene classification, land-use classification using very high resolution (VHR) images, as well as multi- and hyperspectral data.

In our experiments, the proposed method consistently and substantially outperforms baseline methods including PCA, kernel PCA, and other state-of-the-art methods designed for aerial classification in addition to being very fast. We also explain why single layer convolutional networks (while able to produce strongly discriminative features with local reception fields that capture spatial context) are only practical for high resolution (pixel level) fine grain classification, and that deeper networks can also learn to detect more abstract features which enhances their performance.
document.input:
This paper explores the application of single-layer and deep convolutional neural networks for the analysis of remote sensing data. Applying supervised convolutional architectures directly to multi- and hyperspectral imagery is often problematic due to the high dimensionality of the data and the scarcity of labeled training samples. To address these challenges, we propose a greedy layer-wise unsupervised pre-training strategy powered by a highly efficient algorithm for learning sparse features. Our approach is grounded in sparse representation theory and simultaneously enforces both population and lifetime sparsity. We demonstrate the expressive power of these learned representations across several diverse scenarios, including aerial scene classification, land-use mapping in very high resolution (VHR) imagery, and land-cover classification from multi- and hyperspectral datasets. 

In our experiments, the proposed method significantly outperforms standard dimensionality reduction techniques like Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and kernel PCA, as well as several state-of-the-art algorithms for aerial classification, all while maintaining high computational efficiency. Our results indicate that while single-layer convolutional networks can extract potent discriminative features by accounting for spatial context in the receptive field, they are best suited for tasks requiring high-resolution, granular detail. In contrast, deep architectures offer superior performance by capturing increasingly complex levels of abstraction through their hierarchical feature structure.
